perturbation.original_text: "Gross Revenues" means the gross revenues received by the Licensee and derived from or in connection with the Licensee Site, from any sources whatsoever, including but not limited to fees for access to and use of the Licensee Site and advertising, sponsorship, marketing and other paid-for placements, andmaintenance, support and other services.
perturbation.changed_text: "Gross Revenues" means the revenues received by the Licensee. The definition is subject to change based on internal accounting practices of the Licensee.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Gross Revenues' is specific and detailed. The modified definition is vague and allows the Licensee to alter the definition based on internal accounting practices, creating an in-text contradiction because Section 5 depends on a consistent and clear method for calculation. This directly affects royalty payments, creating uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text:
In addition to the License Fee payable in accordance with Section 5.1, Licensee shall pay a royalty ("Royalty") to Licensor according to the following schedule ("Royalty Schedule"):

Subscribers	Royalty Payable as Percentage of Gross Revenue
0 - 5000	6.25%
5001 - 7500	6.75%
7501 - 10,000	7.00%
10,001 - 15,000	8.00%
15,001 - 20,000	8.50%
20,001 - 25,000	9.25%
25,001+	9.75%
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a specific definition for 'Gross Revenues' which is used to calculate royalty payments in Section 5. The changed text makes the definition of 'Gross Revenues' vague and subject to change based on the Licensee's internal accounting practices. This creates a direct contradiction with the Royalty Schedule in Section 5, which relies on a consistent and well-defined 'Gross Revenue' figure for calculating royalty payments. The change introduces uncertainty in enforcement and allows the Licensee to manipulate the basis for royalty calculations, directly conflicting with the intent of Section 5. Therefore, the omission creates an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.3 Terms of Use. Throughout the Term, Licensee shall have in effect and maintain accessible on the Licensee Site and Related Media website terms of use ("Terms of Use") and privacy policy ("Privacy Policy") on which use of the Licensee Site and Related Media, including the Licensed Content, is expressly conditioned, and which in form and substance are reasonably acceptable to Licensor.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3 Terms of Use. Throughout the Term, Licensee may establish terms of use for the Licensee Site and Related Media. The Licensor will be notified of any updates.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates that the Licensee must have Terms of Use and a Privacy Policy reasonably acceptable to the Licensor. The modified clause makes it optional for the Licensee to establish terms of use. By removing the mandatory requirement and the need for Licensor approval, there is an in-text contradiction with section 8.2(c), which requires the Licensee to comply fully with Section 4.3 for indemnification purposes. If 4.3 is no longer a strict obligation, 8.2(c) becomes unenforceable in the original intended manner.
perturbation.contradicted_text:
By Licensee. Licensee shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless Licensor against all Losses arising out of or resulting from any Action by a third party related to or arising out of:
(a) the Licensee Site or Related Media, including any material displayed or services provided thereon but excluding Licensed Content and Marks used in accordance with this Agreement;
(b) Licensee's use of the Licensed Content and/or Licensor's Marks in a manner not permitted by this Agreement (including Licensee's continued use of any Content Item in violation of Section 4.1(d) or any of Licensor's Marks after Licensor has directed Licensee to cease using any such Marks; or
(c) Licensee's failure to comply fully with Section 4.3.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the Licensee to have Terms of Use and Privacy Policy acceptable to the Licensor, while the changed text makes establishing terms of use optional. Section 8.2(c) requires the Licensee's full compliance with Section 4.3 for indemnification. If Section 4.3 becomes optional, as per the changed text, it directly contradicts and undermines the enforceability of the indemnification clause in Section 8.2(c) because the Licensee can no longer be held liable for failing to comply with a mandatory obligation, as that obligation no longer exists. Therefore, it creates a strong in-text contradiction based on the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 11.12 Dispute Resolution. In the event of a dispute arising out of this Agreement, the parties shall first negotiate in good faith in an effort to reach a settlement of the dispute. If having negotiated in good faith, the parties are unable to resolve their dispute, the parties shall submit the dispute to binding arbitration by a single arbitrator. The arbitration shall be governed by the Commercial Rules of Arbitration of the American Arbitration Association. The arbitration shall take place at an agreed location, or if the parties cannot agree on a venue in Reno, Nevada or Vancouver, BC, as determined by the flip of a coin. The arbitrator shall have jurisdiction over the conduct of discovery prior to the hearing. Notwithstanding the foregoing, nothing in this Section 11.13 shall be construed to prohibit either party from seeking appropriate injunctive or other equitable relief in a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.12 Dispute Resolution. In the event of a dispute arising out of this Agreement, the parties will negotiate in good faith to reach a settlement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies a mandatory binding arbitration process if initial negotiations fail. The modified clause removes the mandatory arbitration component. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 11.13, which addresses attorneys' fees in the event of an 'action, suit, or other legal or administrative proceeding'. If arbitration is no longer mandatory, the type of 'proceeding' is undefined, rendering the attorneys' fees clause ambiguous since it assumes a specific legal pathway (arbitration or court) that is no longer guaranteed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11.13 Attorneys' Fees. In the event that any action, suit, or other legal or administrative proceeding is instituted or commenced by either party hereto against the other party arising out of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs from the non-prevailing party.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the mandatory arbitration clause, directly contradicting Section 11.13. Section 11.13 discusses attorneys' fees in the event of an 'action, suit, or other legal or administrative proceeding'. Because arbitration is no longer guaranteed, the type of 'proceeding' is now undefined, creating ambiguity and a direct conflict. The original text ensured a clear legal pathway (arbitration or court), while the modified text creates uncertainty, rendering Section 11.13's application unclear and contradictory to its original intent. This creates a strong contradiction because the removal of mandatory arbitration undermines the enforceability and clarity of the attorneys' fees clause.
